comments.body: I think `kill --all` should mean "kill all processes in the container" instead, unless we have another good name for that flag?

> I think `kill --all` should mean "kill all processes in the container" instead, unless we have another good name for that flag?

isnt that called stop?  --all does have the precedent of being used elsewheres soley for "all containers"

Stop doesn't hit anything except container init, same as kill. All
processes will hit other processes including exec sessions.

On Sep 29, 2018 12:03, "Brent Baude" <notifications@github.com> wrote:

I think kill --all should mean "kill all processes in the container"
instead, unless we have another good name for that flag?

isnt that called stop? --all does have the precedent of being used
elsewheres soley for "all containers"

—
You are receiving this because your review was requested.

Reply to this email directly, view it on GitHub
<https://github.com/containers/libpod/pull/1568#issuecomment-425655839>,
or mute
the thread
<https://github.com/notifications/unsubscribe-auth/AHYHCBldo2if6RGtacHqpsx7OO1WRHKCks5uf5ncgaJpZM4XAjfp>
.

I dissagree.  I think kill all means kill all containers.  That is what I was trying to do when I  noticed it was not working.  I was giving demos of podman with an updated version of @umohnani8 talk, and I wanted to kill all of the containers that were running without waiting for the stop signal to happen.

Just two minor things:

- I think that "**Kill** all running containers" is more comprehensible for users than "**Signal** all running containers". Technically, a kill signal is being sent but I am not sure tha all users are familiar with signal handling and might be confused.

- I would like to add a test for this flag to avoid potential future regressions.
I still want an all processes flag, but you're right that it would be confusing to repurpose --all when it means other things elsewhere. Would like to see a test like @vrothberg said though.
